threats.title: Protocol Insolvency via Uncapped TGT Deposits
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract lacks a cap on total TGT deposits, allowing total claimable TITN to exceed the contract's actual TITN balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: Without a deposit cap, early claimers can drain the contract, leaving late claimers with nothing and causing underflows in post-year withdrawal logic.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; code lacks cap enforcement.
preconditions: Contract holds a fixed amount of TITN (TITN_ARB)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: onTokenTransfer accepts deposits indefinitely as long as time < 360 days

operational_questions: Is there an off-chain mechanism preventing TGT deposits beyond the cap?
What happens to users who attempt to claim after the TITN balance is exhausted?
validation_requirements: Verify claimTitn reverts for late claimers due to insufficient balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Users deposit TGT into MergeTgt over time
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Contract TITN balance reaches zero while totalTitnClaimable > 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None, can happen organically or via malicious whale

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol insolvency; late claimers permanently lose funds
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires admin to deposit additional TITN to cover the shortfall

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Total TGT balance in MergeTgt approaching TGT_TO_EXCHANGE
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed claimTitn transactions due to safeTransfer reverts

threats.title: Permanent Transfer DoS via Cross-Chain Dusting (Tainting Attack)
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can permanently lock a victim's TITN transfers on the destination chain by bridging a dust amount to their address.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _credit function permanently flags any recipient as a bridged token holder, subjecting them to transfer restrictions indefinitely.
threats.config_evidence: Titn.sol _credit function permanently sets isBridgedTokenHolder flag.

proof_of_signal.pattern: isBridgedTokenHolder[_to] = true is set permanently with no unset mechanism

preconditions: Attacker has a small amount of TITN on the source chain
isBridgedTokensTransferLocked is true on the destination chain

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _credit permanently sets isBridgedTokenHolder for any recipient

operational_questions: Can an attacker bridge dust to arbitrary addresses?
Does this permanently lock the victim's transfers?
validation_requirements: Test bridging to a third-party address

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker acquires small amount of TITN on source chain
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls OFT send() with victim's address as recipient
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: LayerZero delivers message, _credit is called on destination
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim's isBridgedTokenHolder flag is set, blocking their transfers

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires admin to globally disable transfer lock or whitelist victim

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed transfer transactions from active users
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: One address bridging to many unrelated addresses

threats.title: Token Loss via Misleading LayerZero Endpoint Transfer Exemption
threats.hypothesis_description: Users attempting to bridge TITN by transferring directly to the LayerZero endpoint (as permitted by the transfer restriction logic) will permanently lose their tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _validateTransfer function explicitly allows transfers to lzEndpoint, but actual OFT bridging requires calling send() which uses _burn. Direct transfers to the endpoint do not trigger bridging and result in stuck tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: to != lzEndpoint exemption allows direct ERC20 transfers to the endpoint address

preconditions: User attempts to bridge by directly transferring to lzEndpoint

operational_questions: Do users attempt to bridge by transferring directly to the endpoint?
Can tokens be recovered from the endpoint?
validation_requirements: Verify OFT send() uses _burn, not transfer
Check if endpoint contract has recovery function

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User reads documentation stating transfers to endpoint are allowed for bridging
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transfer succeeds due to exemption in _validateTransfer
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Tokens are stuck in endpoint contract, bridging does not occur

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High, depends on endpoint contract upgradeability/recovery features

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Direct ERC20 transfers to lzEndpoint address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users complaining about missing bridged tokens

threats.title: Arithmetic Underflow in Post-Year Withdrawals Permanently Locks Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: If the contract's TITN balance is less than totalTitnClaimable at the 360-day mark, the withdrawRemainingTitn function will permanently revert due to an arithmetic underflow.
threats.investigation_rationale: The calculation (remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable) assumes the contract balance is always >= total claimable. If this is violated (via over-allocation or admin withdrawal), all post-year claims are permanently bricked.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable underflows if balance < claimable

preconditions: Contract TITN balance < totalTitnClaimable at 360 days

initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None directly, but admin withdraw can trigger it

operational_questions: Can the contract balance fall below total claimable?
Does the underflow permanently brick the function?

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Contract balance falls below totalTitnClaimable (via over-deposit or admin withdraw)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts due to underflow, locking all remaining claims

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires admin to deposit TITN to fix the balance before the first successful call

threats.title: Proportional Share Truncation Leaves Dust Permanently Locked
threats.hypothesis_description: Integer division in the proportional share calculation causes the sum of all distributed shares to be slightly less than the total unclaimed TITN, leaving dust locked in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because userProportionalShare is calculated per user with truncation, the residual dust from each calculation accumulates and cannot be withdrawn by anyone.
preconditions: initialTotalClaimable does not evenly divide (claimableTitn * unclaimedTitn)

operational_questions: Does truncation leave dust in the contract?

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Each user's share is rounded down due to integer division
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: After all users claim, a small amount of TITN remains locked in the contract

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires admin to use withdraw() to recover dust

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Small TITN balance remaining after all claims

threats.title: TGT transferAndCall Spoofing Misattributes Claimable Balances
threats.hypothesis_description: If the external TGT token allows a caller to specify an arbitrary 'from' address during transferAndCall, an attacker can misattribute claimable TITN balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: The onTokenTransfer function blindly trusts the 'from' parameter provided by the TGT contract. A vulnerable or non-standard ERC677 implementation could allow spoofing this parameter.
deployment_assumptions: TGT token implements transferFromAndCall or similar allowing arbitrary 'from'

proof_of_signal.pattern: Trusts 'from' parameter without verifying caller's relationship to 'from'

preconditions: TGT token implements transferFromAndCall or similar allowing arbitrary 'from'

operational_questions: Does the TGT token allow arbitrary 'from' addresses in transferAndCall?

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker identifies vulnerability in TGT token's transferAndCall
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls transferAndCall with victim's address as 'from'
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: MergeTgt credits TITN to victim instead of attacker
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker disrupts attribution or exploits logic depending on victim's state

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users receiving claimable TITN without depositing TGT

threats.title: Off-by-One Boundary Condition Causes Loss of Deposited TGT
threats.hypothesis_description: A user depositing TGT at exactly 360 days after launch will have their TGT transferred into the contract but will receive 0 claimable TITN.
threats.investigation_rationale: onTokenTransfer uses '> 360 days' to block deposits, while quoteTitn uses '< 360 days' to calculate output. At exactly 360 days, the deposit is allowed but the output is 0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Mismatch between > 360 days and < 360 days at exact 360 day mark

preconditions: User deposits exactly at block.timestamp == launchTime + 360 days

operational_questions: Can a user deposit at exactly 360 days?
validation_requirements: Verify TGT is taken and 0 TITN credited

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User attempts to deposit TGT at exactly block.timestamp == launchTime + 360 days
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User loses TGT and receives 0 claimable TITN

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires admin to manually refund TGT

threats.title: Admin Rug Pull via Unrestricted withdraw Function
threats.hypothesis_description: The owner can call the withdraw function to drain all TITN and TGT from the contract, leaving users with no ability to claim or recover funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The withdraw function has no restrictions on which tokens can be withdrawn or how much, completely breaking the trust model and solvency invariant.
threats.config_evidence: MergeTgt.sol withdraw function has no restrictions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Owner can withdraw any token including deposited TITN

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: withdraw function allows arbitrary token extraction

operational_questions: Is there a timelock or multisig?
validation_requirements: Verify admin can drain TITN and TGT

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin key is compromised or admin turns malicious
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Contract is drained, users cannot claim or recover funds
